english: Din Tai Fung Earl Grey Lemon Tea

region_of_origin: Singapore (served at Din Tai Fung Singapore); Earl Grey tea originates from the United Kingdom

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301103). No specific Earl Grey lemon tea term exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KL covers tea infusions broadly. No specific Earl Grey lemon variant code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to tea as a substance. No specific Earl Grey lemon tea concept exists; closest_parent used.

main_ingredients: Earl Grey tea (black tea with bergamot oil)
ice (made from the same brewed beverage)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup or glass of 300 ml of brewed Earl Grey lemon tea, as typically served at Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355); Lemon juice, raw (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 09152)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for Earl Grey lemon tea as a combined beverage. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed black tea (NDB 14355) and raw lemon juice (NDB 09152), blended in an approximate 95:5 ratio by volume to reflect a lightly lemon-accented tea. Energy, carbohydrate, and sugar values are slightly elevated above plain brewed tea to account for the lemon component. Sodium and mineral values follow the brewed tea baseline. This is a best-available estimation from approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 (Atkinson et al.) — plain tea and low-carbohydrate beverages convention
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed reference: Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Earl Grey lemon tea. Plain brewed tea contains negligible fermentable carbohydrates and is conventionally assigned a GI of effectively zero in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) to satisfy numeric field requirements. gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is below 1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (approximately 3 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Earl Grey lemon tea is very low in sodium (approximately 9 mg per 300 ml serving) and poses no meaningful blood-pressure concern; individuals with hypertension may consume it freely, provided no sugar or high-sodium additives are included.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and sugar content; effectively zero glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With fewer than 2 g of carbohydrate per 300 ml serving and a glycemic load of effectively zero, Earl Grey lemon tea has no clinically meaningful impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol; black tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Earl Grey lemon tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and provides bergamot-derived and black-tea polyphenols that have been associated with modest improvements in LDL cholesterol and endothelial function in observational studies; it is a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed unsweetened.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Earl Grey Lemon Tea (伯爵柠檬茶) is a brewed beverage made from Earl Grey black tea — a variety of black tea flavoured with bergamot orange oil — combined with lemon juice or lemon flavouring, served chilled over ice. At Din Tai Fung Singapore, the drink is distinguished by the use of ice cubes made from the same brewed tea, ensuring that the flavour remains consistent and undiluted as the ice melts. The result is a refreshing, lightly citrus-accented tea with a floral bergamot aroma, a clean tannic backbone, and a bright lemon finish. Nutritionally, the beverage is extremely low in calories (approximately 2 kcal per 100 ml), contains negligible protein and fat, and provides only trace carbohydrates from the lemon component. Sodium content is very low at approximately 3 mg per 100 ml. The beverage contributes a small amount of potassium and vitamin C from the lemon. Its glycemic load per serving is effectively zero, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose, hypertension, or cardiovascular risk. The polyphenols in black tea, including theaflavins and thearubigins, as well as bergamot flavonoids, have been studied for potential antioxidant and lipid-modulating effects. This drink is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible when prepared without added sugar or dairy.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Earl Grey lemon tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a combined product. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed black tea and raw lemon juice in an approximate ratio. The adaptation is methodologically sound and uses approved sources, but the absence of an exact match limits confidence to medium.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: brewed tea is an aqueous infusion with no meaningful protein content; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy NDB 14355 (brewed black tea, protein 0.0 g per 100 g). 2. fat_g is naturally zero: brewed tea contains no lipids; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy NDB 14355. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are naturally zero for the same reason. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: tea is a liquid infusion with no dietary fibre; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero for a plant-derived beverage with no animal components. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero; tea contains no vitamin D. 8. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 based on USDA SR Legacy values for brewed tea which are at or below detection threshold; treated as confirmed zero. 9. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea. 10. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 11. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 12. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 13. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 14. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 15. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 16. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 17. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 18. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate per serving is below 1 g. 19. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact ontology term for Earl Grey lemon tea exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 20. Price data from field survey data (SGD 7.41) is preserved as context but not used for nutritional derivation. 21. The dish is classified as Singaporean-served Chinese cuisine; multilingual naming follows the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
